title: Topological Sort
description: Ordering tasks so every dependency comes before what depends on it: DFS plus a stack, and why it only works on a DAG.
objectives: Understand the problem topological sort solves: ordering with dependencies
Implement topological sort using DFS and a stack
Understand why postorder push produces the correct order when reversed
Know why topological sort requires a directed ACYCLIC graph

sections.text: 1. Recap: DFS, for a Specific New Purpose
Graph DFS explored a graph fully. Topological sort uses that exact same DFS mechanism for a specific goal: producing an order where every dependency comes before whatever depends on it.
2. The Problem: Ordering Tasks with Dependencies
A topological sort of a directed graph is an ordering of its vertices such that for every directed edge from A to B, A appears before B in the ordering.
3. The Approach: DFS Plus a Stack

sections.title: The core idea
steps: Run DFS from every unvisited vertex
The moment a vertex finishes exploring ALL its neighbors (recall Postorder Traversal's timing), push it onto a stack
After all DFS calls finish, pop the stack — that popped order IS the topological order

sections.text: A vertex only gets pushed after everything it depends on (recall Graph DFS diving into dependencies first) has already finished and been pushed — so popping the stack (LIFO, recall Stack) naturally reverses that, putting dependencies first.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: dfs(0): visits 0, then dives into 1. dfs(1) dives into 3. dfs(3) has no unvisited neighbors → pushes 3 first
Back in dfs(1): no more neighbors → pushes 1
Back in dfs(0): next neighbor is 2. dfs(2)'s neighbor 3 is already visited → pushes 2
Back in dfs(0): no more neighbors → pushes 0
Stack, bottom to top: 3, 1, 2, 0. Popping gives: 0, 2, 1, 3 — a valid order (0 before 1 and 2; 1 and 2 before 3)

sections.text: Topological sort only makes sense on a directed acyclic graph (DAG) — recall Graphs' cycle definition. If course A depends on course B, and course B depends on course A, there's no valid order at all; this contradiction is exactly what a cycle represents. Detecting whether a graph even has a valid topological order requires checking for cycles, a refinement beyond this introduction.

sections.title: Getting dressed in a valid order
sections.text: You must put on socks before shoes, and a shirt before a jacket — but socks and a shirt have no dependency on each other, so either could come first. Any valid "getting dressed" sequence that respects every such rule is a topological sort of the dependencies; a rule saying "shoes before socks" AND "socks before shoes" simultaneously would make getting dressed genuinely impossible — exactly a cycle.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Pushing a vertex to the stack too early, before all its neighbors are done — must happen at the very end of the DFS call, postorder-style.
Forgetting to run DFS from every unvisited vertex — a graph might not be fully reachable from a single starting point.
Assuming topological sort works on any directed graph — it requires no cycles; a cyclic graph has no valid topological order at all.
Forgetting the result must be read by popping the stack, not by the order things were pushed — pushing happens in reverse-dependency order.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does a topological sort produce?
questions.answer: An ordering of a directed graph's vertices such that for every edge from A to B, A appears before B in the ordering.

questions.question: How does the DFS-based topological sort algorithm work?
questions.answer: Run DFS from every unvisited vertex, pushing each vertex onto a stack only after all of its neighbors have finished being explored; popping the stack at the end gives the topological order.

questions.question: Why must the graph be acyclic for a topological sort to exist?
questions.answer: A cycle means two vertices depend on each other, directly or indirectly, making it impossible to place either one consistently before the other — no valid ordering can exist.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Topological sort orders a directed graph's vertices so every dependency appears before what depends on it, solving problems like course prerequisites or task scheduling. The DFS-based approach runs DFS from every unvisited vertex, pushing each vertex onto a stack only after all of its neighbors are fully explored — postorder timing — then reading the result by popping the stack, which naturally places dependencies first. This only works on a directed acyclic graph (DAG): a cycle would mean two vertices mutually depend on each other, making a consistent ordering impossible.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Topological Sort
sections.text: Topological sort = getting dressed in valid order, socks before shoes. DFS pushes a vertex AFTER its dependencies finish (postorder). Pop the stack to read the correct order. Needs a DAG — no contradictory cycles.
